learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate awareness of the basic principles, concepts and policies used in urban planning.
Develop a working knowledge of key planning data sets, data platforms, quantitative and qualitative measures used in spatial planning and urban design.
Apply spatial planning, urban design and other analytical frameworks and produce professional planning reports.
Work effectively independently, and with others, to investigate a planning problem, evaluate opinions, generate ideas, design and justify solutions.
Develop basic skills in the spatial analysis, statutory planning, urban design and placemaking methods.
Effectively design and communicate original ideas, solutions and recommendations to complex planning problems
Establish an appreciation of the technical capabilities, professional ethics and values of planners, including those standards and competencies established by professional planning associations.

assessments.assessment_title: Assessment 1: Local Planning Report and Poster
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale:

For this assessment, you will be accessing planning data from Queensland, Australia and investigating a location based on publicly available data and insights generated by students attending the field trip. Students will form teams for this project. This assessment has three parts, that each team must prepare and submit:

  • Part 1: Local Planning Report (2500 words including annotated maps and diagrams. PDF format)
  • Part 2: Project Plan including Teamwork Agreement, Activity Log and Peer Assessments (PDF format)
  • Part 3: Poster - A1 size (digital file, PDF format)

This is an introductory project in the collection, collation, analyse and use of data in a planning project. Students will use overlay mapping (sieve mapping) to synthesise baseline information and succinctly communicate the constraints and opportunities of an identified site for renewal or redevelopment. Details on the structure and content of each part of this assessment will be provided in Blackboard and discussed in class.

If, for whatever reason, you find that your group is not functioning effectively, please contact your Course Coordinator for support.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Assessment 2: Urban Design Analysis Slide Deck
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale:

This is an introductory project in urban design analysis. As an individual assessment activity, students are required to select and analyse three precincts or urban spaces using an agreed urban design framework. One of the precincts/spaces should be in the nominated inner-city location identified by our industry partner. Students will propose design strategies for each precinct, that improve the ‘place qualities' and user experience. Students are to submit their analysis as well as process diary, in the format of two short video presentations:

Presentation 1: Urban Design Analysis Slide Deck (video presentation with 20 slides) suitable for Pecha Cucha style presentation (i.e. timed 5 minute video presentation) together with slide deck and script.

Presentation 2: Process Slide Deck (video presentation with 3-4 slides) reflecting on your sites, approach to precinct analysis, use of AI and any other challenges with the design process (i.e. timed 1- 3 minute video presentation). Note: This component of the assessment is used to contribute to achievement of Identity Verified Assessment Hurdle requirements.

Planners are increasingly being required to use this highly visual reporting format to communicate their analysis and ideas. Guidance on presentation structure and content, and how to upload your video presentations will be provided in Blackboard.

Student's individual progress on this assessment will be monitored and recorded at in-person consultations at the scheduled weekly practical sessions. In addition, each week students will be randomly selected to share and talk to their work in progress via an 'informal' update to the class. Students who fail to share their work in progress and/or fail to provide an update as requested - may not meet the Identity Verified Assessment requirements for this course. Students must attend a minimum of 80% of the practicals and have their progress recorded as part of the urban design module to meet course requirements.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale:

The objective of this online exam is to demonstrate a knowledge and understanding of a range of planning issues, frameworks and skills including but not limited to matters observed or discussed over the semester. 

The exam will consist of:

  • Case Study Activity requiring the use of online government mapping platforms to critically analyse a site/planning issue. 
  • A reflection on the planning and professional skills and knowledge developed through the semester.

Students will have the opportunity to complete a mock exam, to familiarise themselves with the digital exam format and timed conditions.

In-person assessment prohibits use of AI or MT: This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: Exams submitted after the end of the submission time will incur a late penalty.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Planning Skills: Minjerribah Field Trip

MINJERRIBAH FIELD TRIP: A compulsory 2-day (overnight) field trip will be held in Week 3. The field trip will visit the Minjerribah (North Stradbroke Island) and Redland City local government areas. Students will develop an understanding of place and community contexts through the collection of data at the local scale and engagement with stakeholders. Activities will be focussed on the themes of sustainability, inclusion and resilience in urban planning and development.

See Blackboard for more information.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

learning_activities.topic:
Urban Design Module: Inner City Urban Design Walk

Urban Design Walk: Students will take part in a guided urban design walk of a nominated suburb or precinct. Students will travel independently to the site (via public transport) and meet at a nominated location. The walk will occur within the timetabled lecture and practical sessions (Week 8).

See Blackboard for more information.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05, L07
